summary: Gruber uses the analogy of high-end bicycles and cameras to frame Mark Pilgrim's decision to switch from Mac OS X to Ubuntu Linux. He argues that evaluating 'better' is inherently subjective and multi-dimensional — what matters depends on which criteria you value. Pilgrim's central concern is openness and long-term data preservation, not that Mac OS X has a bad UI. Gruber criticizes Mac users who dismiss Pilgrim's argument out of hand, comparing their dogmatism to Windows users who dismiss the Mac's UI advantages. He concedes Pilgrim has valid points about Apple's calculated indifference to openness, while noting that if Apple's lack of openness were truly disastrous, the disaster would have already occurred after 22 years.
key_points: Evaluating 'better' is subjective and multi-dimensional — a cheap bike can be better than an expensive one depending on your needs, and the same applies to operating systems
Most commenters missed Pilgrim's central argument about openness and long-term data preservation, instead fixating on trivial points about backups and UI quality
There's an unbecoming tendency among Mac users to deny that any other OS could be better in any way, mirroring how Windows users dismiss Mac UI advantages
Apple's stance on openness appears highly calculated — open only so far as necessary to be perceived as being open, a marketing stance rather than an engineering one
The strongest counter-argument to Pilgrim is that if Apple's lack of openness were truly catastrophic, it would have manifested already in his 22 years of use
money_quotes: It's generally pretty easy for most people to perceive the difference between the mediocre and the good, but it often requires expertise or finely-honed sensibility to perceive the difference between the good and the great.
"Better" is a complicated adjective, because it is subjective.
Just because evaluating "better-ness" is subjective does not mean it is arbitrary.

I'm deeply suspicious of Mac users who claim to be perfectly happy with Mac OS X. Real Mac users, to me, are people with much higher standards, impossibly high standards, and who use Macs not because they're great, but because they suck less than everything else.

Telling Pilgrim that he's making a mistake because Ubuntu doesn't have as refined or cohesive a UI as Mac OS X is like telling someone who is switching from a Chevy Tahoe to a Toyota Prius that he's not going to have as much cargo room. He knows it.
Apple's stance on openness is in fact highly calculated, and that the calculation is that Apple should be open only so far as necessary to be perceived as being open.
An ideology is an organized system of beliefs; just because you don't share them doesn't mean they aren't valid.
"Better" for Mark Pilgrim doesn't mean better for you, and nowhere has Pilgrim implied that it does.
key_insight: Dismissing someone's informed decision to leave your preferred platform, rather than engaging with their actual arguments, reveals the same dogmatism you accuse the other side of having.
